title: Common Verbs

examples.kp: Munta ya i Elena king eskwela.

entries.notes: Root for movement away from speaker. Past: minta. Used with future or habitual aspect. Cf. mako (to leave/go away).
Root: datang. Past completed: dintang. The initial d- shifts to y- in some affixed forms.
Root: lakad. Infinitive/actor-focus: lumakad. Shared root with Tagalog lakad.

examples.kp: Takbu na ka, e ka malambat!

entries.notes: Root: takbu. Actor-focus: tumakbu. Related to Tagalog takbo.

examples.kp: Matudtud na ku — ing tudtud ku malambat.
examples.en: I am going to sleep — my sleep takes long.

entries.notes: Root: tudtud. Matudtud = to sleep (going to sleep). E ku mipapatudtud = I cannot sleep.

examples.kp: Lukluk la reng tau king silya.
examples.en: The people sat on the chairs.

entries.notes: Root: lukluk. Luklukan = chair (the thing one sits on). Well-attested in Kapampangan sources.
Root: tikdo. Confirmed in Kapampangan proverbs and Glosbe Swadesh list. Means 'to stand' (distinct from tikdo = wake up in some regional variants; the dominant meaning per Glosbe is 'stand').

examples.en: I will give the book to him.

examples.kp: Ibie ke ing susi kang Carmen.
examples.en: I will give the key to Carmen.

entries.notes: Also spelled ibiye. Patient-focus verb for giving. Ibie ke = I will give (it). Common in everyday and literary Kapampangan.
Root: kunan. Used for taking or getting an object. Attested in Kapampangan phrase lists.

examples.en: I will buy fruit at the market.

entries.notes: Also seen as seli in some sources. Root: sali/seli. Confirmed in multiple Kapampangan word lists and the Tagaloglang dictionary.
One of the most frequently confirmed Kapampangan verbs. Buri daka = I like you. Used for both liking and wanting. Distinct from sintâ (adoration) and ibug (craving).

examples.en: He is happy in his work.

entries.notes: Root: obra (from Spanish obra). Verbal form: magobra. Confirmed in Kapampangan story texts and phrasebooks.
Root: sabi. Sabian = patient-focus 'to say/tell'. Confirmed in Kapampangan grammar sources and Wikipedia examples.
Root: gewa (variant of gawa). Confirmed in Kapampangan phrase lists and grammar examples. Nanu ing gagewan mu = What are you doing/making?
Root: lawen. Confirmed in Tagaloglang word list. Used for both looking at and watching something.

examples.en: His writing is pleasant to read.
I am writing a letter to him.

entries.notes: Root: sulat. Shared with Tagalog. Susulat = is writing; sumulat = will write; sinulat = wrote. Widely confirmed in multiple sources.

examples.kp: Mamasa la ri Maria at Juan.

examples.en: I read a book every day.

entries.notes: Root: basa. Mamasa = ongoing/habitual actor-focus for read. Confirmed in multiple Kapampangan grammar and vocabulary sources.

examples.en: I have great love for her.

examples.kp: Ala nang lugud king puso na.
examples.en: There is no more love in his heart.

entries.notes: Root noun for reciprocal love. Kaluguran daka = I love you (expression with ka-...-an circumfix). Lugud implies mutual affection; one-sided longing is sintâ. Confirmed across multiple Kapampangan cultural and linguistic sources.

examples.en: I am sad when you leave.

entries.notes: Verb meaning 'to leave / go away / be absent'. Often used in conditional or temporal clauses: 'nung miras ka' (when you are away). Contrast with 'munta' (to go toward a destination) — 'miras' emphasizes departure from the current place.

Verb meaning 'to beat/throb', used almost exclusively with 'puso' (heart): 'Tibuk ya ing puso ku' (My heart is beating/throbbing). Equivalent to Tagalog 'tibok ng puso'. The root form 'tibuk' appears as a predicate followed by the 3sg enclitic 'ya'.

examples.kp: Ding ilug palwal la king dagat.
examples.en: The rivers flow out to the sea.

entries.notes: Verb describing the flow of rivers into a body of water — 'to flow out into / empty into'. Used as a predicate: 'palwal la' (they flow out), where 'la' is the 3rd person plural enclitic. Typically used with rivers and water bodies.

examples.kp: Ing kambing makmak ya ning damu.
examples.en: The goat grazes on the grass.

entries.notes: Verb meaning 'to graze' — used for animals eating grass or grazing in a field. The subject is typically a grazing animal (goat, carabao, horse). 'makmak ya ning damu' = grazes on the grass ('ning' is the oblique article for the grass).

lessons.title: On the Move
lessons.intro: Learn the three most common movement verbs: munta (go), datang (come/arrive), and lakad (walk).

exercises.prompt: Munta ya i Elena king eskwela.
accepted: elena will go to school.
elena will go to school
the man arrived.
the man arrived
the man has arrived.
the man has arrived

exercises.prompt: Lumakad ya papunta king bale. → Root verb: ____ (to walk)

lessons.title: Body & Rest
lessons.intro: Learn action verbs for the body: takbu (run), tudtud (sleep), lukluk (sit), and tikdo (stand).

accepted: i am going to sleep.
i am going to sleep
i am going to sleep already.
i am going to sleep already
i'm going to sleep now.
i'm going to sleep now
i will sleep.
i will sleep
sit here.
sit here

exercises.prompt: Tumakbu ya ing anak. → Root verb: ____ (to run)

lessons.title: Give & Take
lessons.intro: Learn exchange verbs: ibie (give), kunan (take/get), and sali (buy).

accepted: i will give the book to him.
i will give the book to him
i will give the book to her.
i will give the book to her
i will buy fruit at the market.
i will buy fruit at the market

exercises.prompt: Sige, ____ ke. (OK, I will take it.)
____ ke ing susi kang Carmen. (I will give the key to Carmen.)

lessons.title: Work & Will
lessons.intro: Learn three powerful everyday verbs: buri (want/like), obra (work), and gewa (do/make).

accepted: she likes that.
she likes that
he likes that.
he likes that
i haven't done anything wrong.
i haven't done anything wrong
i have not done anything wrong.
i have not done anything wrong

exercises.prompt: Magobra ya aldo-aldo. → Root verb: ____ (to work)

lessons.title: Words & Eyes
lessons.theme: Saying, looking, and the written word
lessons.intro: Learn communication and perception verbs: sabian (say/tell), lawen (look/watch), sulat (write), and mamasa (read).

accepted: tell me.
tell me
i am writing a letter to him.
i am writing a letter to him
i am writing a letter to her.
i am writing a letter to her

exercises.prompt: Susulat ku liham. → Root verb: ____ (to write)

lessons.title: The Language of the Heart
lessons.intro: Kapampangan has a rich vocabulary for love. This lesson focuses on lugud, the word for reciprocal, mutual love.

exercises.prompt: Ala nang lugud king puso na.
accepted: there is no more love in his heart.
there is no more love in his heart
there is no more love in her heart.
there is no more love in her heart
there is no more affection in his heart.
there is no more affection in his heart
there is no more affection in her heart.
there is no more affection in her heart
there is no more passion in his heart.
there is no more passion in his heart
there is no more passion in her heart.
there is no more passion in her heart
i have great love for her.
i have great love for her
i have great love for him.
i have great love for him
i have great affection for her.
i have great affection for her
i have great affection for him.
i have great affection for him
i have great passion for her.
i have great passion for her
i have great passion for him.
i have great passion for him

exercises.prompt: Ala nang ____ king puso na. (There is no more love in his heart.)
Dakal ing ____ ku kaniya. (I have great love for her.)

lessons.title: More Verbs: Leave, Beat, Flow, Graze
lessons.intro: 'miras' means to leave or go away. 'tibuk' is the beat of the heart. 'palwal' describes rivers flowing into the sea. 'makmak' is what goats and carabaos do — graze on grass.

accepted: i am sad when you leave
i am sad when you leave.
i am sad when you are away
i am sad when you are away.
i'm sad when you leave
i'm sad when you leave.
my heart is beating
my heart is beating.
my heart beats
my heart beats.

exercises.prompt: Ding ilug ____ la king dagat. (The rivers flow out to the sea.)
Ing kambing ____ ya ning damu. (The goat grazes on the grass.)

sources: Wikipedia: Kapampangan language — https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kapampangan_language (verb examples: munta, datang/dintang, buri, sulat, sumulat, sinulat, kaluguran)
Wikivoyage Kapampangan Phrasebook — https://en.wikivoyage.org/wiki/Kapampangan_phrasebook (munta, datang, lakad, obra)
Glosbe Pampanga-English Dictionary (tikdo=stand, tudtud=sleep) — https://glosbe.com/pam/en/tikdo and https://glosbe.com/tl/pam/matulog
Polytranslator English–Kapampangan Dictionary — https://www.polytranslator.com/dictionary/kapampangan/ (munta, datang, lakad, takbu, turu/tudtud, sali, lako, buri, obra, sabian, sulat, basa, lugud)
Common Kapampangan Words & Phrases (pdfcoffee/idoc.pub) — community phrasebook (matudtud, lukluk, sali/seli, kunan, ibie/ibiye, kaluguran, gewa, lawen, mamasa, magsalita, sabian)
bryanquiroz22.wordpress.com — Filipino Dialects: Kapampangan Grammar and Vocabulary (sulat conjugations: susulat/sumulat/sinulat; seli; gawa; sabian; ume; munta)
siuala.com — Kapampangan words for love: ibug, burî, sintâ, mal, lugud — https://siuala.com/kaluguran/ (confirms lugud = mutual love noun, kaluguran daka = I love you)
sunstar.com.ph — Garcia: Ing Pamanudtud Kapampangan (confirms tudtud root for sleep; matudtud na ku)
josephjames007.blogspot.com — Common Phrases in Kapampangan (matudtud, sali, kaluguran, buri)
Forman, Michael L. (1971). Kapampangan Dictionary. University of Hawai'i Press. [Referenced via JSTOR and Google Play Books for root word confirmation]
confidenceNotes: HIGH confidence (attested in 3+ independent sources): munta (go), datang/dintang (come/arrive), lakad (walk), sulat (write), buri (want/like), obra (work), sabian (say/tell), lugud (love-noun), sali (buy), lukluk (sit), mamasa (read). MEDIUM confidence (attested in 2 sources): takbu (run — confirmed by Polytranslator and Wikipedia grammar notes), tudtud (sleep — Glosbe + sunstar.com), tikdo (stand — Glosbe Swadesh list + proverb in Wikipedia), ibie (give — confirmed with example sentence from 2+ sources), kunan (take/get — confirmed with example sentence), gewa (do/make — confirmed with example sentence), lawen (look — Tagaloglang + sunstar.com). EXCLUDED due to insufficient verification or conflict: tagse (run, only one source), turu (sleep variant, less attested than tudtud), magsalita (speak, mostly Tagalog-influenced form), lako (sell — only one Kapampangan source). NOTE: lugud is listed as partOfSpeech 'noun' as it is primarily a noun root (mutual love); kaluguran is the verb phrase derived from it but is in the avoid-list. The headword 'lugud' is NOT in the avoid-list.
